title: zod declared as dep but schemas are hand-rolled — worst of both worlds
description: package.json declares 'zod: ^3.22.0' as a runtime dependency, yet schemas/path.ts and schemas/phases.ts hand-roll ~120 lines of validation. The only zod consumer is src/index.ts:14 which uses z.string() for a single MCP tool input parameter. The contract permits hand-rolled OR zod; choosing both pays the dep cost without the simplicity payoff. A 4-field path schema and a 3-level phases schema in zod would be ~10 lines each vs. the current 60 + 65.
impact: Extra dep in the install graph; reviewer/maintainer must context-switch between two validation idioms. If zod is kept, schemas should use it; if hand-rolled is the choice, drop zod and inline a 3-line type-guard for projectRoot.
recommendation: Pick one path. Either (a) rewrite path.ts/phases.ts using z.object/z.array (the schemas are trivially expressible), or (b) drop the zod dep and replace z.string() in index.ts with an inline `typeof projectRoot === 'string'` guard.

title: scanRegistry is async but uses only sync fs APIs
description: scanRegistry is declared `async` and returns Promise<RegistryResult>, but its body uses fs.readdirSync, fs.existsSync, and fs.readFileSync exclusively — there is no await inside. The async wrapper exists to match runStart's await, but it adds a microtask round-trip and obscures that the function is pure-sync. Same pattern in probeOutputStyle (outputStyle.ts:17 — async with only readFileSync inside).
impact: Misleading signature. Readers expect await points; finding none, they wonder what they missed. Trivial perf overhead from the unnecessary Promise wrapping.
recommendation: Either (a) drop `async` and let callers `await` a sync return (works fine — await on a non-Promise is a no-op), or (b) switch to `fs.promises.readdir/readFile/access` to match the async signature. Option (a) is the smaller diff.

title: existsSync + readFileSync double-stats every path.json
description: For each candidate path dir, the code calls fs.existsSync(pathJsonFile) and then fs.readFileSync(pathJsonFile). The second call already throws ENOENT if the file is gone, so existsSync is redundant — and introduces a TOCTOU race that does nothing useful here but is a bad reflex pattern. The two distinct warning kinds ('missing-path-json' vs. 'malformed-path-json') can both be derived from the readFileSync catch by inspecting err.code === 'ENOENT'.
impact: Two stat() syscalls per path instead of one. More importantly, the reader has to verify that the second call's failure mode is reachable when existsSync succeeds (it is — permissions, race) and that the warning kinds remain distinct.
recommendation: Replace the existsSync gate with a single try/catch around readFileSync. Branch on err.code === 'ENOENT' for the missing-path-json case; everything else becomes malformed-path-json (read-error variant).

title: Harness 'boots MCP server' but only switches on tool name in-process
description: bootHarness is documented (contract A12) as 'boots the MCP server and successfully invokes start'. The implementation never instantiates McpServer, never connects a transport, and contains a single switch branch (`if (toolName === 'start')`) that calls runStart directly. The throw on unknown tool is unreachable in cycle 1 (start is the only tool registered). HarnessInstance interface, BootOptions interface, and the no-op shutdown() add ceremony around what is effectively a one-line wrapper over runStart.
impact: The harness misrepresents itself: tests think they exercise the MCP transport surface, but they bypass it entirely. Cycle 2-5 scenarios that depend on actual MCP wire behavior will need a rewrite. Today's complexity is also dishonest — the interfaces imply a non-trivial harness.
recommendation: Either (a) actually spawn the MCP server via stdio and use the SDK's Client to call tools (a few extra lines, but matches the name), or (b) rename to `inProcessDispatcher` and drop the HarnessInstance/BootOptions ceremony — just export `callStart(projectRoot)` returning the same shape. The contract says 'boot the MCP server'; option (a) is the honest read.

title: PathInfo and PathData are structurally identical, defined twice
description: registry.ts defines `PathInfo` with five fields (slug, title, summary, personalization_options: string[], build_command). schemas/path.ts defines `PathData` with the same five fields (slug, title, summary, personalization_options: PersonalizationOption[], build_command). The only nominal difference is `string[]` vs the narrower `PersonalizationOption[]`, and PersonalizationOption is itself just a subset of string. registry.ts then also explicitly re-spreads validation.value field-by-field (lines 109-115) instead of `paths.push(validation.value)`.
impact: Duplicate type definition; readers must verify the two stay in sync. Future field additions need three edits (interface, interface, the explicit copy in scanRegistry).
recommendation: Export PathData from schemas/path.ts as the single source of truth and have registry.ts re-export it as PathInfo (or just use PathData everywhere). Replace lines 109-115 with `paths.push(validation.value)`.

title: RegistryWarning declares unused optional `dir` field
description: RegistryWarning has both `path?: string` and `dir?: string` optional fields. The implementation only ever sets `path` (lines 68, 81, 92, 104). `dir` is never written to. It's a speculative field — either reserved for a future iteration that isn't specified, or vestigial from an earlier draft.
impact: Schema noise. Consumers of the warning shape can't tell whether `dir` is meaningful or dead. Tests that grep for warning shape may inadvertently match on a field the engine never produces.
recommendation: Drop `dir?: string` from the interface. If a future cycle needs it, add it then.

title: Triple cast to Record<string, unknown> for the same parsed value
description: After narrowing `parsed` to a non-null object, the code casts it to Record<string, unknown> three separate times in the same expression chain (line 50, line 51, line 56). Each cast is `(parsed as Record<string, unknown>)['enabledPlugins']`. A single intermediate const would eliminate two of the three casts and read more naturally.
impact: Minor readability. The `as` casts also signal to a reader that type narrowing isn't being trusted, when in fact the `typeof parsed !== 'object' || parsed === null` guard above already rules out non-records.
recommendation: After the null/object guard, do `const parsedObj = parsed as Record<string, unknown>;` then reference `parsedObj.enabledPlugins` once. Or use `'enabledPlugins' in parsed` followed by direct property access on the narrowed type.
evidence:
!('enabledPlugins' in parsed) ||
    typeof (parsed as Record<string, unknown>)['enabledPlugins'] !== 'object' ||
    (parsed as Record<string, unknown>)['enabledPlugins'] === null

title: Field-by-field copy where `paths.push(validation.value)` would suffice
description: validatePath returns `value: PathData` with exactly the five fields PathInfo needs. The code then re-constructs the object field-by-field. Since PathData and PathInfo are structurally identical (R4-005), this could be `paths.push(validation.value)` — one line instead of seven. The current form invites bugs when fields are added (you must remember to extend the copy block).
impact: Seven lines instead of one. Adds a maintenance footgun: a field added to PathData/PathInfo without updating this block silently drops it from the registry output.
recommendation: Replace lines 109-115 with `paths.push(validation.value);`. Combine with R4-005 by unifying PathData and PathInfo.

title: Harness boot-time projectRoot vs. per-call projectRoot is unused flexibility
description: bootHarness takes a `projectRoot` in BootOptions, then the inner callTool checks `args['projectRoot']` and falls back to the boot-time value. The contract A12 and T-028 only require a single fixture root. No test or caller passes a different projectRoot per call. This is speculative parameterization — two ways to specify the same input.
impact: Reader has to reason about which projectRoot wins. Cycle 1 needs neither — just take projectRoot at call time and skip the boot-options indirection.
recommendation: Either pass projectRoot only at call time, or only at boot. Pick one. Given the harness is meant to model in-process server invocation, boot-time is fine; drop the per-call override.
evidence: const root = typeof args['projectRoot'] === 'string' ? args['projectRoot'] : projectRoot;
